name: Pyridoxine (Vitamin B6)
contraindications: Avoid prolonged high-dose self-supplementation due to neuropathy risk
Use medication review when combined with anticonvulsants or levodopa regimens

sources.title: Vitamin B6 Fact Sheet for Health Professionals (NIH ODS)

description: Vitamin B6 includes pyridoxine, pyridoxal, pyridoxamine, and phosphorylated forms, with pyridoxal-5-phosphate as the active coenzyme form in many reactions. The research corpus frames supplementation around adequacy, deficiency prevention, and selected investigational use cases with mixed outcomes.
summary: Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) is an essential cofactor nutrient used primarily to maintain adequacy and prevent deficiency, with cautious supplemental use because chronic high doses can cause neuropathy.
mechanism: Proposed/established mix: B6-dependent enzymes support amino-acid metabolism and neurotransmitter synthesis pathways (including serotonin and GABA-related pathways). Proposed symptomatic benefits outside deficiency correction remain condition specific and not definitive.
safetyNotes: RDA-level intake is generally well tolerated.
Chronic high supplemental intake (commonly above 100 mg/day) is associated with sensory peripheral neuropathy; symptoms can improve after discontinuation.
interactions: High-dose pyridoxine can alter response to certain anticonvulsants (for example phenytoin).
Can affect levodopa metabolism when levodopa is not protected with a decarboxylase inhibitor.
dosage: Supplement use in the literature often spans 50–100 mg/day, but higher chronic dosing increases neuropathy risk.
UL context: approximately 100 mg/day for adults.
preparation: Widely available as pyridoxine hydrochloride in single-ingredient products and B-complex formulations.
regulatoryStatus: Essential vitamin with established dietary reference intakes; broadly available as a dietary supplement ingredient.
